myth_text_en: Vaccines cause infertility in women.
correct_text_en: No, vaccines do NOT cause infertility. They are rigorously tested for safety. Protecting your child with vaccines saves lives and has no effect on fertility.
correct_text_lg: Enkingo tezikosa kubeera n'abaana. Zikezesebwa bulungi ennyo.
symptoms: Vaccine-preventable diseases: High fever, paralysis (polio), rash (measles), difficulty breathing (whooping cough).
prevention: Follow the Uganda National Expanded Programme on Immunisation (UNEPI) schedule from birth.
treatment: Vaccines prevent disease. If your child misses a dose, visit the nearest health center immediately for catch-up immunization.

myth_text_en: Vaccines contain microchips to track people.
correct_text_en: No, vaccines contain NO microchips. They contain weakened or killed germs, proteins, or mRNA that train your immune system to fight disease. Microchip technology cannot survive injection.
correct_text_lg: Enkingo tezirimu chip. Zirimu obuwuka obutakkivu oba pulootiini ezinya omubiri okutangaala.
symptoms: Vaccine-preventable illnesses cause severe fever, paralysis, deafness, and death in unvaccinated children.
prevention: Vaccinate all children on schedule. Vaccines are free at government health facilities in Uganda.
treatment: Once a child contracts measles or polio, only supportive care is available — prevention through vaccination is far safer.

myth_text_en: Only sick children need vaccines.
correct_text_en: Fact: ALL healthy children need vaccines on schedule. Routine immunization prevents Polio, Measles, TB, and Hepatitis B before a child is ever exposed.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abaana bonna baetaaga enkingo nga balamu. Okugemba bulijjo kuziyiza mumpumpu, olukusu, TB n'endwadde nnyingi.
symptoms: Unvaccinated children are at high risk of fever, paralysis, pneumonia, and death from preventable diseases.
prevention: Complete all 8 vaccines in the UNEPI schedule: BCG, OPV, Pentavalent, PCV, Rotavirus, IPV, MR, and Yellow Fever.
treatment: Vaccine-preventable diseases have no cure — only supportive treatment. Prevention is the only reliable solution.

myth_text_en: HIV is caused by witchcraft.
correct_text_en: HIV is NOT caused by witchcraft. It is caused by a virus (HIV) that attacks the immune system. While there is currently no cure, it is managed through ART which allows for a healthy life. Prevention is through ABC (Abstinence, Be Faithful, Condoms).
correct_text_lg: HIV si mulogo. Ereeterwa akawuka. Eddagala lya ART liyamba, naye tewali ddagala liwonya.
symptoms: Fever, weight loss, and recurring infections.
prevention: Use condoms, avoid sharing needles, and get tested regularly.
treatment: Lifelong ART (Antiretroviral Therapy).

myth_text_en: There is no medicine to prevent HIV after exposure.
correct_text_en: Fact: PrEP and PEP are medicines that can prevent HIV infection if taken as directed by a health worker.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Waliwo eddagala (PrEP/PEP) eriziyiza HIV ssinga okikozesa mu ddwaliro.
detailed_guidance_en: PEP must be started within 72 hours of exposure. PrEP is for people at high risk who are currently negative.
detailed_guidance_lg: PEP erina kutandikibwa mu ssaawa 72 oluvannyuma lw'okusisinkana akawuka. PrEP eyaabo abali mu buzibu obungi naye nga tebalina kawuka.

myth_text_en: Eating sugarcane or too much sun causes malaria.
correct_text_en: Fact: Malaria (Omusujja gw'ensiri) is only caused by the bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Omusujja gw'ensiri gureetebwa nsiri, si ebikajjo oba musana.
symptoms: High fever, chills, sweating, headache, nausea, and muscle pain.
prevention: Sleep under a treated mosquito net, use insect repellent, and clear stagnant water.
treatment: Malaria is cured with Coartem (ACTs) after a positive blood test. Never self-medicate.

myth_text_en: Sleeping under a net makes you feel too hot and isn't necessary.
correct_text_en: Fact: Sleeping under a Long-Lasting Insecticide-Treated Net (LLIN) is the most effective way to prevent malaria.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Okusula mu katimba k'ensiri akalimu eddagala kwekuziyiza omusujja gw'ensiri okusinga.
symptoms: Fever, headache, and joint pain.
prevention: Consistent use of LLINs, indoor residual spraying, and wearing long clothes at night.
treatment: Tested and treated at any health facility using ACTs.

myth_text_en: Hospital delivery is dangerous — home birth is safer.
correct_text_en: No, facility-based delivery is far safer. Uganda's maternal mortality is largely due to home births without skilled care. Hospitals have equipment to handle emergencies like bleeding and obstructed labour.
correct_text_lg: Okuzaala mu ddwaliro kusinga okuteeka omukazi n'omwana mu mbeera ennungi. Okufa kw'abakazi ab'olubuto mu Uganda kuzisibwa okuzaala ewaka.
symptoms: Danger signs in pregnancy: Heavy vaginal bleeding, severe headache, blurred vision, convulsions, baby not moving, high fever.
prevention: Attend all 8 ANC visits. Deliver at a certified health facility. Have a birth plan ready including transport to the hospital.
treatment: If complications arise, go to the nearest health facility immediately. Call the Emergency Maternal Hotline: 0800-100-066.

myth_text_en: You only need to visit the clinic once during pregnancy.
correct_text_en: Fact: At least 8 ANC visits are required by MOH Uganda. These visits detect anaemia, high blood pressure, infections, and foetal positioning — all of which can be life-threatening if missed.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Omukazi alina okukeberebwa emirundi munaana (8) ng'ali lubuto. Okukebera kuziyiza anemia, pulesha, n'endwadde ez'omwana.
symptoms: Missed ANC can result in undetected anaemia, pre-eclampsia, or malpositioned baby — all dangerous during delivery.
prevention: Register for ANC in the first trimester (before 12 weeks). Bring your antenatal card to every visit.
treatment: ANC visits include iron/folic acid tablets, tetanus injections, malaria prevention, and HIV testing.

myth_text_en: Drinking hot water or steam kills COVID-19.
correct_text_en: No, drinking hot water does NOT kill COVID-19. The virus lives in your respiratory tract, not your stomach. Drinking boiling water only causes burns to your mouth and throat.
correct_text_lg: Okunywa ebyokulya ebyokya tekutuuka mu mawuggwe gye kawangaalira. Akawuka kaba mu nkanu, si mu lubuto.
symptoms: COVID-19: Dry cough, fever, loss of taste or smell, tiredness, and difficulty breathing.
prevention: Get vaccinated, wash hands with soap regularly, wear a mask in crowded places, and ventilate indoor spaces.
treatment: Rest, drink plenty of fluids, and take paracetamol for fever. Go to a health facility if breathing becomes difficult.

myth_text_en: COVID-19 is a spiritual curse or caused by 5G towers.
correct_text_en: No, COVID-19 is caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus — a biological pathogen, NOT 5G signals or a curse. It spreads through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs, sneezes, or talks.
correct_text_lg: COVID-19 ereetebwa kawuka (virus), si mpewo za 5G oba bulogo. Ekyetwalibwa ku mpewo ez'olusu.
symptoms: Fever, dry cough, loss of smell/taste, headache, body aches, and in severe cases, difficulty breathing.
prevention: Vaccination, hand hygiene, masking, social distancing, and good ventilation in enclosed spaces.
treatment: Mild: rest and fluids at home. Severe: seek immediate medical care with oxygen support.

myth_text_en: Eating mangoes causes malaria.
correct_text_en: Malaria is caused by mosquito bites, not fruit.
correct_text_lg: Okulya emmiyembe tekuleta musujja gw'ensiri.

myth_text_en: Malnutrition only happens to poor families.
correct_text_en: Fact: Malnutrition can happen to anyone. A balanced diet with proteins, carbs, and vitamins prevents stunting.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Endiisa ennungi eyetaagibwa buli muntu okuziyiza okukula obubi.

myth_text_en: Ebola is a curse from spirits.
correct_text_en: No, Ebola is a deadly viral disease spread ONLY through direct contact with body fluids of an infected or deceased person. It is NOT spiritual. Isolation and PPE are critical for containment.
correct_text_lg: Ebola si mulogo. Esaasaanyizibwa mu kukwata ku mazzi g'omubiri gw'omulwadde oba omufu.
symptoms: Sudden fever, severe headache, muscle pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and unexplained bleeding.
prevention: Avoid contact with body fluids of sick or deceased persons. Wash hands thoroughly. Alert the nearest health center immediately.
treatment: Immediate isolation at a certified Ebola Treatment Unit. Do NOT attempt home treatment. Call the emergency line: 0800-100-066.

myth_text_en: Having sex with a virgin cures HIV.
correct_text_en: No, that is NOT true. Having sex with a virgin does NOT cure HIV. HIV has no cure, and such acts only spread the virus further and harm innocent people. HIV is managed only by ART.
correct_text_lg: Nga, ekyo si kituufu. Okwesisinkanya n'omuwala omuto tikuwonya HIV. HIV tawona naye ajjanjabwa na ART.
symptoms: Fever, fatigue, and night sweats.
prevention: Safe sex, partner testing, and avoiding shared sharp objects.
treatment: Strict adherence to ART medication.

myth_text_en: Mosquitoes can transmit HIV from one person to another.
correct_text_en: Fact: Mosquitoes CANNOT transmit HIV. The virus cannot survive inside a mosquito, and they do not inject blood from a previous person into the next.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Ensiri teziyisaamu HIV. Akawuka kafa mangu ssinga kaba mu nsiri.
symptoms: N/A (Related to transmission myth)
prevention: Focus on sexual transmission and blood contact, not insects.
treatment: N/A

myth_text_en: Cholera is caused by eating too much food.
correct_text_en: No, Cholera is caused by the Vibrio cholerae bacteria found in contaminated water or food. Eating too much food does not cause Cholera. It can kill within hours if untreated.
correct_text_lg: Cholera ava ku buwuka mu mazzi amacaafu oba emmere erimu obubi, si okulya ennyo.
symptoms: Profuse watery diarrhea (like rice-water), vomiting, and rapid dehydration.
prevention: Drink boiled or treated water only. Wash hands with soap. Use clean latrines and safe food handling.
treatment: Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) immediately. Seek IV fluids at a health facility for severe cases. Antibiotics prescribed by a doctor.

myth_text_en: STDs are caused by sitting on dirty latrines.
correct_text_en: No, STIs are caused by bacteria, viruses, or parasites transmitted through UNPROTECTED SEXUAL CONTACT. A toilet seat is not a route of transmission for any major STI.
correct_text_lg: Endwadde z'ekikaba ziva mu kwegatta okutali kukuume, si kuteekebwa ku kabuyonjo.
symptoms: Unusual discharge (penis/vagina), sores or rashes in genital area, painful urination, and lower abdominal pain.
prevention: Consistent and correct condom use, mutual monogamy, regular STI screening, and partner notification.
treatment: Visit a health facility for testing and a full course of antibiotics or antivirals as prescribed.

myth_text_en: Condoms are not effective and contain holes.
correct_text_en: Correctly used condoms are 98% effective at preventing STIs and HIV. Claims of factory holes are false — condoms are rigorously tested before distribution.
correct_text_lg: Obupiira bukozesebwa bulungi buziyiza endwadde z'ekikaba n'HIV okutuuka ku 98%.
symptoms: Without condom use, STIs may cause sores, discharge, pain, and can lead to infertility or death.
prevention: Use a new condom for every sexual encounter. Condoms are available free at all government health facilities.
treatment: Condoms prevent transmission. If symptoms appear, visit a health facility for diagnosis and treatment immediately.

myth_text_en: Syphilis is just a minor skin rash that goes away on its own.
correct_text_en: Syphilis (Kabotongo) is a dangerous bacterial infection. It has 4 stages and can cause permanent brain, heart, and nerve damage if left untreated. It requires Penicillin injections.
correct_text_lg: Kabotongo lulwadde lwa buwuka oluluma ennyo. Lwetaaga eddagala lya Penicillin, tolweka bwokka.
symptoms: Stage 1: Painless sore. Stage 2: Body rash, fever, hair loss. Stage 3: No symptoms. Stage 4: Brain damage, paralysis, death.
prevention: Condom use, faithful partnerships, and regular STI testing especially during pregnancy.
treatment: Penicillin injection (Benzathine penicillin G) prescribed by a doctor. Do not self-medicate.

myth_text_en: Gonorrhea is caused by standing in the rain or cold.
correct_text_en: No, Gonorrhea (Nanziri/Enziku) is caused by the bacteria Neisseria gonorrhoeae, transmitted ONLY through sexual contact. Rain and cold have no role in its transmission.
correct_text_lg: Enziku oba Nanziri lulwadde lwa buwuka era lusaasaanyizibwa mu kwegatta, si musana oba nkuba.
symptoms: Thick yellow-green discharge, burning urination, and in women: no symptoms (silent infection) or pelvic pain.
prevention: Condom use and regular STI screening. Partners must be treated simultaneously to prevent re-infection.
treatment: Dual antibiotic therapy (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin) as prescribed by a doctor.

myth_text_en: Chlamydia has no symptoms so it cannot be dangerous.
correct_text_en: Chlamydia is called a 'silent infection' because 70% of women show NO symptoms, yet it can permanently damage fallopian tubes and cause infertility. It requires a lab test to detect.
correct_text_lg: Chlamydia eyinza obutalaga bubonero naye eyinza okureetera omukazi obutazaala.
symptoms: Often none. If present: discharge, burning urination, or lower abdominal pain. In men: discharge from penis.
prevention: Routine STI screening (especially for sexually active women under 25), condom use, and partner treatment.
treatment: Azithromycin or Doxycycline antibiotics as prescribed. Both partners must be treated.

myth_text_en: Hepatitis B is only spread through shared food or saliva.
correct_text_en: Hepatitis B is 100x more infectious than HIV. It spreads through blood, unprotected sex, and mother-to-child during birth. It is NOT spread through food, water, coughing, or hugging.
correct_text_lg: Hepatitis B asaasaanyizibwa mu musaayi, kwegatta, oba okuva ku nnyina okujja omwana. Si ku mmere oba okuyogera.
symptoms: Jaundice (yellow eyes/skin), dark urine, extreme fatigue, nausea, and abdominal pain. Chronic infection can lead to liver cancer.
prevention: Vaccination (3-dose HepB vaccine) is the most effective prevention. Safe sex and avoid sharing needles or razors.
treatment: No cure, but antiviral medications control the virus. Vaccination prevents infection entirely.

myth_text_en: Genital warts are caused by skin irritation or poor hygiene.
correct_text_en: Genital warts (Ensekere) are caused by HPV (Human Papillomavirus), the most common STI worldwide. Certain HPV strains cause cervical cancer in women. The HPV vaccine prevents the most dangerous strains.
correct_text_lg: Ensekere ziva ku kawuka ka HPV era kiyinza okureetera kookolo w'omumwa gw'ennabaana.
symptoms: Soft, flesh-colored bumps in the genital area. Often painless but may itch. Cervical cancer from HPV may be asymptomatic until advanced.
prevention: HPV vaccination (girls aged 10-14), consistent condom use, and cervical cancer screening (VIA/Pap smear) for women.
treatment: Warts can be treated (frozen, burned, or removed) but the virus stays in the body. Cervical cancer requires surgery or chemotherapy.

myth_text_en: Trichomoniasis is caused by poor hygiene alone.
correct_text_en: Trichomoniasis (Trich) is caused by a tiny parasite Trichomonas vaginalis, transmitted through sexual contact. It is the most common CURABLE STI worldwide but requires proper medication.
correct_text_lg: Trichomoniasis ava ku kawuka k'ekika kya parasite era awoneka bulungi n'eddagala.
symptoms: Women: Yellow-green frothy discharge with fishy odor, itching, painful urination. Men: Often no symptoms.
prevention: Condom use and treating both partners simultaneously.
treatment: A single dose of Metronidazole (400-500mg) taken by both partners. Avoid alcohol during treatment.

myth_text_en: Malaria is caused by general mosquitoes.
correct_text_en: Fact: Malaria is specifically caused by the Plasmodium parasite transmitted by the female Anopheles mosquito.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Omusujja gw'ensiri guvva ku kawuka ka Plasmodium akasaasaanyizibwa ensiri ya Anopheles.

myth_text_en: Male circumcision has no health benefits.
correct_text_en: Fact: Voluntary Medical Male Circumcision (VMMC) reduces the risk of female-to-male HIV transmission by approximately 60%.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Okukomola abasajja mu ddwaliro kukendeeza akatyabaga k'okufuna HIV n'ebitundu 60%.

myth_text_en: Babies need water in addition to breast milk from birth.
correct_text_en: Fact: Breast milk alone provides all the water and nutrients a baby needs for the first 6 months. Giving water can cause diarrhea and reduce milk intake.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Amabeere ga nnyina gakka ge getaagibwa omwana okumala emyezi 6. Okuwa amazzi kiyinza okuleeta olummeeme.
symptoms: Infant dehydration/malnutrition: Sunken fontanelle, dry mouth, fewer than 6 wet diapers per day, lethargy, poor weight gain.
prevention: Exclusive Breastfeeding (EBF) for the first 6 months. Initiate breastfeeding within 1 hour of birth. Feed on demand.
treatment: For dehydration or diarrhea in infants under 6 months, seek professional clinical assessment. Continue frequent exclusive breastfeeding.

myth_text_en: Babies can eat adult food at 3 months.
correct_text_en: Fact: Complementary feeding should begin at exactly 6 months. Start with soft mashed foods like matooke, sweet potato (lumonde), or porridge (obushera) alongside continued breastfeeding.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Emmere ey'okwongerako etandikibwa ku myezi 6. Tandika n'emmere empola nga matooke, lumonde, oba obushera ng'osigala okuyonsa.
symptoms: Early complementary feeding risks: Diarrhea, vomiting, severe stomach pain, choking, allergic reactions, weight loss.
prevention: Begin complementary feeding at exactly 6 months. Continue frequent breastfeeding up to 2 years or beyond.
treatment: Introduce one food at a time to monitor allergies. For persistent diarrhea or vomiting, seek immediate pediatric care.

myth_text_en: Eggs are bad for young children and cause worms.
correct_text_en: Fact: Eggs are one of the most nutritious foods for children. They provide protein, iron, and Vitamin A essential for brain development and growth. They do NOT cause worms.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Amagi ge bimu ku birungo ebisinga obulungi eri abaana. Biwa pulootiini, eyaani, ne Vitamin A ebiyamba obwongo n'okukula.
symptoms: Protein-energy malnutrition (PEM) symptoms: Edema (swollen feet/face), thinning hair, extreme thinness, listlessness, growth stunting.
prevention: Feed children aged 6 months and older one egg daily. Ensure proper cooking (hard-boiled) to prevent bacterial infections.
treatment: Nutritional support containing high-quality proteins like eggs, groundnuts, and milk. Deworm children every 6 months starting at 1 year.

myth_text_en: Short children are just taking after their parents genetically.
correct_text_en: Fact: Stunting (being too short for age) is usually caused by chronic malnutrition, not genetics. A diverse diet with proteins, vegetables, and fruits prevents stunting.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Okuba omukutte mu baana kiseera ekisinga kireeterwa obutafuna mmere nnungi, si bigenderengo. Endiisa eyenjawulo eziyiza okukutta.
symptoms: Stunting indicators: Low height-for-age, delayed motor skill milestones, frequent illnesses, poor school performance.
prevention: Ensure maternal balanced nutrition during pregnancy. Practice exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months and introduce a diverse complementary diet.
treatment: Early intervention with nutrient-dense foods, vitamin A supplementation, and routine monitoring of child growth milestones.

myth_text_en: Dried silver fish (mukene) is inferior to imported food.
correct_text_en: Fact: Mukene (silver fish) is a superfood rich in calcium, protein, and omega-3 fatty acids. It is excellent for children's bone and brain development.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Mukene y'ekyokulya eky'amaanyi ennyo ekirimo calcium, pulootiini, n'amafuta ga omega-3. Kiyamba amagumba n'obwongo bw'abaana.
symptoms: Calcium deficiency in children: Weak bones, rickets (bowed legs), muscle cramps, delayed tooth eruption.
prevention: Incorporate ground mukene powder into children's porridge (obushera) and meals regularly starting at 6 months.
treatment: Daily intake of calcium-rich foods like mukene and milk. Clinical calcium and Vitamin D supplementation for diagnosed rickets.

myth_text_en: Vitamin A supplements are unnecessary for children.
correct_text_en: Fact: Vitamin A deficiency is a leading cause of preventable blindness in Ugandan children. Orange-fleshed sweet potato, carrots, mangoes, and dark green vegetables like dodo (amaranth) are excellent sources.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Obutaba na Vitamin A kureetera obuzibu bw'amaaso mu baana. Lumonde ow'alangi, kaloti, emiyembe, ne dodo bifuna Vitamin A nnyingi.
symptoms: Vitamin A deficiency: Night blindness (difficulty seeing in dim light), dry eyes (xerophthalmia), frequent respiratory and skin infections.
prevention: Ensure children receive Vitamin A capsules every 6 months at health facilities or during Child Health Days.
treatment: Immediate high-dose Vitamin A administration at a clinic for children showing signs of xerophthalmia or severe measles.

myth_text_en: Teenagers can survive on just posho and beans.
correct_text_en: Fact: Adolescents need a diverse diet including proteins (eggs, fish, beans), iron-rich foods (dodo, liver), fruits, and vegetables to support rapid growth and brain development.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abavubuka beetaaga endiisa eyenjawulo nga amagi, ebyenyanja, ebijanjaalo, dodo, ekibumba, ne bibala okuyamba okukula n'obwongo.
symptoms: Adolescent nutritional gaps: Poor school performance, fatigue, slow height increase, recurrent skin breakouts, weakened immunity.
prevention: Incorporate local protective foods (fruits like pawpaw, orange) and proteins (mukene, eggs) alongside staple posho/beans.
treatment: Provide balanced daily meals. Ensure twice-yearly school-based or community-based deworming and micronutrient checks.

myth_text_en: Only pregnant women need iron supplements.
correct_text_en: Fact: Adolescent girls lose iron through menstruation. Foods like liver, mukene, dodo (amaranth), beans, and fortified flour help prevent anemia.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abawala abavubuka bafiirwa eyaani mu nsonga z'omwezi. Emmere ng'ekibumba, mukene, dodo, n'ebijanjaalo ziyamba okuziyiza anemia.
symptoms: Anemia in teenage girls: Extreme tiredness, pale inner eyelids/palms, dizziness, shortness of breath, cold hands/feet.
prevention: Consume iron-rich foods weekly. Under the MOH Uganda program, receive weekly Iron-Folic Acid (IFA) supplements in school.
treatment: For confirmed anemia, take daily therapeutic iron tablets under health worker supervision for 3 months.

myth_text_en: Rolex (chapati and egg roll) and sodas are a balanced meal.
correct_text_en: Fact: While rolex provides some protein and carbs, a balanced meal also needs vegetables, fruits, and clean water. Limit sugary drinks and fried snacks to prevent obesity and diabetes.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Newankubadde rolex eyamba ku pulootiini, emmere ennungi nayo yeetaaga enva, ebibala, n'amazzi amalungi. Kendeeza ku bikomye n'ebinywe eby'amasukali.
symptoms: Junk-heavy diet consequences: Excessive weight gain (obesity), sluggishness, dental cavities, elevated blood sugar.
prevention: Replace sodas with clean boiled water or fresh local fruit juice. Add tomatoes, onions, and cabbage into chapati rolls.
treatment: Nutrition counseling, increased daily physical activity, and establishing regular balanced home meal schedules.

myth_text_en: Pregnant women should eat less to have a small baby for easy delivery.
correct_text_en: Fact: Pregnant women need MORE food — at least one extra meal per day. Eating less causes low birth weight and increases risk of death for both mother and baby.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abakazi ab'olubuto beetaaga emmere ENNYO — okulya ekyokulya eky'okwongera buli lunaku. Okulya ekitono kuleeta omwana omutono era nga n'akabi kapulikirizibwa.
symptoms: Inadequate pregnancy intake: Maternal weight loss, severe lethargy, fetal growth restriction, high susceptibility to infections.
prevention: Eat at least three balanced meals and one extra snack daily. Partner support in sourcing diverse local foods.
treatment: Enroll in the antenatal nutrition program. Targeted food supplementation for pregnant mothers with low body mass index.

myth_text_en: Iron and folic acid tablets during pregnancy are harmful.
correct_text_en: Fact: Iron and Folic Acid (IFA) tablets are essential during pregnancy to prevent anemia and birth defects. Take them as prescribed by your health worker.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Amabaale ga Iron ne Folic Acid geetaagibwa mu lubuto okuziyiza anemia n'obuzibu mu kuzaala. Gaminya nga omuweereza w'obulamu bw'akugambye.
symptoms: Iron/folate deficiency in pregnancy: Severe pallor, swelling of feet, neural tube birth defects in the infant, premature birth.
prevention: Take one IFA tablet daily throughout pregnancy starting from the first antenatal clinic (ANC) visit.
treatment: High-dose oral iron therapy or intravenous iron infusions in clinics if maternal hemoglobin drops below 7g/dL.

myth_text_en: Pregnant women should not eat eggs, fish, or meat.
correct_text_en: Fact: Pregnant women NEED protein-rich foods like eggs, fish (tilapia, mukene), meat, beans, and groundnuts for the baby's brain and organ development.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abakazi ab'olubuto BEETAAGA emmere erimu pulootiini ng'amagi, ebyenyanja (ngege, mukene), ennyama, ebijanjaalo, n'ebinyeebwa okuyamba omwana okukula.
symptoms: Protein deficit in pregnancy: Slow fetal growth, postpartum hemorrhage risk, muscle wasting in the mother, poor lactation prep.
prevention: Eat at least one source of animal protein (egg, fish, meat) or two plant proteins (beans, groundnuts) daily.
treatment: Provide maternal nutritional supplements. Target high-risk mothers with fortified corn-soy blend (CSB) foods in local health centers.

myth_text_en: Eating matooke alone is enough during pregnancy.
correct_text_en: Fact: A pregnant woman should eat from ALL food groups daily: energy foods (matooke, posho, rice), body-building foods (beans, fish, eggs), and protective foods (fruits, vegetables like nakati, dodo).
correct_text_lg: Fact: Omukazi alina olubuto alya emmere ey'ebika byonna: eby'amaanyi (matooke, posho), ebyomubiri (ebijanjaalo, ebyenyanja, amagi), n'ebikuuma (bibala, enva ng'enakati, dodo).
symptoms: Micronutrient deficiency in pregnancy: Brittle nails, night blindness, bleeding gums, high susceptibility to common illnesses.
prevention: Create a daily 'colorful plate' featuring yellow/orange fruits, green leafy vegetables, and whole grains.
treatment: Clinical prescription of multiple micronutrient supplements (MMS) and comprehensive nutrition education during ANC sessions.

myth_text_en: Breastfeeding mothers don't need to eat more food.
correct_text_en: Fact: Breastfeeding mothers need an extra 500 calories per day. Eat more porridge (obushera), groundnut paste, fruits, vegetables, and drink plenty of clean water to produce enough milk.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abakyala abayonsa beetaaga emmere eyongerevu. Lya obushera, obunyeebwa obusiikiriddwa, bibala, enva, era nywangako amazzi amalungi okusobola okufuna amabeere amala.
symptoms: Lactation gaps/maternal depletion: Low breast milk volume, extreme maternal fatigue, rapid weight loss, bone aches.
prevention: Eat five times a day (3 meals and 2 snacks). Consume high-calcium foods and drink water before every feed.
treatment: Provide lactation counseling. Clinical supplementation with calcium and multivitamin therapies if depletion is severe.

myth_text_en: Breast milk alone is not enough for a baby — they also need porridge from 2 months.
correct_text_en: Fact: WHO recommends exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months. No water, no porridge, no other foods. Breast milk contains everything the baby needs.
correct_text_lg: Fact: WHO eraga okuyonsa bwokka okumala emyezi 6 egy'olubereberye. Tewali mazzi, tewali bushera, tewali kyokulya. Amabeere galina byonna omwana by'ayetaaga.
symptoms: Early feeding complications: Diarrhea in infants, severe stomach bloating, reduction in maternal breast milk supply, baby rejecting the breast.
prevention: Ensure EBF for the first 6 months. Maintain skin-to-skin contact and frequent breastfeeding.
treatment: For low milk supply, consult a midwife or lactation specialist. Treat infant diarrhea with clinical ORS immediately.

myth_text_en: Eating a lot of one food is better than eating small amounts of many foods.
correct_text_en: Fact: A balanced diet means eating from different food groups every day: carbohydrates (posho, matooke, rice), proteins (beans, fish, eggs), fats (groundnuts, oil), vitamins (fruits, vegetables).
correct_text_lg: Fact: Endiisa ennungi kwe kulya emmere ey'ebika bingi buli lunaku: amaanyi (posho, matooke), pulootiini (ebijanjaalo, ebyenyanja), amafuta (ebinyeebwa), ne vitaminze (bibala, enva).
symptoms: Monotonous diet issues: Micronutrient malnutrition, general lethargy, dry skin, weak hair, slow healing of cuts.
prevention: Use the 'healthy plate' concept: half plate vegetables/fruits, quarter protein, quarter starch.
treatment: Dietary diversification. Incorporate local orange-fleshed sweet potatoes and green dodo leaves daily.

myth_text_en: Drinking tea and soda is the same as drinking water.
correct_text_en: Fact: Adults should drink at least 8 glasses of clean water daily. Tea and soda contain caffeine and sugar which can cause dehydration rather than hydration.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abantu abakulu balina okunywa gilasi munaana (8) ez'amazzi amalungi buli lunaku. Ccayi ne soda biramu caffeine n'amasukali ebireetera omubiri okukala.

myth_text_en: Adding extra salt to food makes it healthier.
correct_text_en: Fact: Too much salt causes high blood pressure (hypertension). Adults should consume less than 5 grams (one teaspoon) of salt per day.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Omunnyo omungi guleeta pulesha ey'amaanyi (high BP). Abantu abakulu balya omunnyo gutasukka kakijiko kamu (5g) olunaku lwonna.

myth_text_en: Old people don't need much food because they are not active.
correct_text_en: Fact: Elderly people need protein-rich foods (eggs, beans, mukene, milk) to maintain muscle strength and prevent falls. They also need calcium and Vitamin D for bone health.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abakadde beetaaga emmere erimu pulootiini (amagi, ebijanjaalo, mukene, amata) okukuuma ebinywa. Era beetaaga calcium ne Vitamin D ey'amagumba.

myth_text_en: Elderly people should drink less water to avoid frequent urination.
correct_text_en: Fact: Dehydration is a serious risk for elderly people. They should drink water regularly even if not thirsty. Dehydration can cause confusion, falls, and kidney problems.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Okukala kw'omubiri kulabe ennyo mu bakadde. Balina okunywa amazzi buli kiseera wadde nga tebanyoomba. Okukala kuyinza okuleeta okutabukana, okugwa, n'obuzibu mu nsigo.

myth_text_en: Elderly people should only eat porridge.
correct_text_en: Fact: While soft foods are easier to chew, elderly people still need diverse nutrition. Mashed beans, soft fish, eggs, ripe bananas, avocado, and enriched porridge provide complete nutrition.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Newankubadde emmere empola nyangu okusoma, abakadde basigala beetaaga endiisa eyenjawulo. Ebijanjaalo ebisiikiriddwa, ebyenyanja ebigonvu, amagi, menvu, avocado, n'obushera obugimu.

myth_text_en: People with HIV don't need special nutrition — ARVs are enough.
correct_text_en: Fact: People living with HIV need 10-30% MORE calories than uninfected people. Good nutrition strengthens the immune system and helps ARVs work better. Eat proteins, fruits, and vegetables daily.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abantu abalina HIV beetaaga emmere ENNYO okusinga abalala 10-30%. Endiisa ennungi enyweza omubiri era eyamba eddagala (ARVs) okukola obulungi.

myth_text_en: You cannot take ARVs on an empty stomach.
correct_text_en: Fact: Most ARVs can be taken with or without food. However, eating regularly helps reduce side effects like nausea. Never skip your medicine because you haven't eaten.
correct_text_lg: Fact: ARVs ezisinga oziyinza okunywangako wadde nga toliiyo. Naye okukuba emmere kuyamba okukendeeza obubonero obubi. Tovaamu kunywa ddagala lyo kubanga tolidde.

myth_text_en: People with diabetes should stop eating all carbohydrates.
correct_text_en: Fact: Diabetics need controlled portions of complex carbohydrates (whole grains, sweet potato, brown posho). Avoid refined sugar and sugary drinks, but don't eliminate carbs entirely.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Abalina ssukaali mu musaayi balya emmere ey'amaanyi empola (lumonde, posho ow'obuwunga obutayongeddwamu). Wewale ssukaali n'ebinywe eby'amasukali, naye tokoma bikyafu byonna.

myth_text_en: Diabetics cannot eat any fruits.
correct_text_en: Fact: Most fruits are safe for diabetics in moderate portions. Choose low-sugar fruits like guava (mapera), oranges, passion fruit, and watermelon. Avoid fruit juices with added sugar.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Ebibala ebisinga birungi eri abalina ssukaali mu musaayi mu bitundu ebitono. Londa ebibala ebirimu ssukaali entono ng'amapera, emicungwa, katunda, ne watermelon.

myth_text_en: Herbal medicine alone can cure diabetes.
correct_text_en: Fact: There is no herbal cure for diabetes. Proper diet, exercise, and prescribed medication (insulin or metformin) are the proven ways to manage blood sugar levels.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Tewali ddagala ly'ebyobuwangwa eliwonyeza ssukaali mu musaayi. Endiisa ennungi, okuddukana, n'eddagala (insulin oba metformin) bye biyamba okubuuzaabuuza ssukaali mu musaayi.

myth_text_en: High blood pressure is only treated with medicine.
correct_text_en: Fact: Diet changes significantly reduce high blood pressure. Eat more fruits, vegetables, potassium-rich foods (bananas, avocado), reduce salt, avoid excessive alcohol, and exercise regularly.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Okukyusa endiisa kuyamba ennyo okukendeeza ku pulesha. Lya bibala, enva, emmere erimu potassium (menvu, avocado), kendeeza ku munnyo, era ddukana buli lunaku.

myth_text_en: If food doesn't taste salty, it has no salt.
correct_text_en: Fact: Many processed and packaged foods contain hidden salt (sodium). Maggi cubes, soy sauce, and processed meats are high in sodium even if they don't taste very salty.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Emmere engi egulwa mu kadduuka erimu omunnyo ogukise (sodium). Maggi, soy sauce, n'ennyama enzigulibwa mu kadduuka biramu sodium nnyingi wadde nga tebibeera munnyo ennyo.

myth_text_en: Imported food is always more nutritious than local food.
correct_text_en: Fact: Local Ugandan foods are highly nutritious. Matooke, groundnuts, beans, dodo (amaranth), nakati, mukene, cassava leaves (kisakye), and orange-fleshed sweet potato provide excellent balanced nutrition.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Emmere ey'eggwanga ly'Uganda eyamu ennyo. Matooke, ebinyeebwa, ebijanjaalo, dodo, enakati, mukene, kisakye, ne lumonde ow'alangi biwa omubiri byonna bye gwetaaga.

myth_text_en: Leftover food is safe to eat the next day without reheating.
correct_text_en: Fact: Bacteria multiply rapidly in cooked food left at room temperature. Always store leftovers in a clean, covered container and reheat thoroughly before eating.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Obuwuka bweyongera mangu mu mmere etekeddwa eyirisiddwa ku bbugumu bw'ekisenge. Bulijjo tereka emmere ey'ejobudde mu kibbo ekyogeddwa era gikumye, era gibuugumye bulungi ng'onnaddayo okulya.

myth_text_en: Reusing cooking oil many times is not harmful.
correct_text_en: Fact: Reheating cooking oil creates harmful compounds (trans fats) linked to heart disease and cancer. Use fresh oil and avoid reheating oil more than twice.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Okuddamu okubuugumya amafuta g'okufumbiramu kuleeta ebintu ebibi (trans fats) ebireetera endwadde z'omutima ne kookolo. Kozesa amafuta amapya era toddayo kubuugumya mirundi esukka ebiri.

myth_text_en: Children don't need breakfast before going to school.
correct_text_en: Fact: Breakfast is the most important meal for school children. It improves concentration, memory, and academic performance. A simple breakfast of porridge, fruit, and an egg is sufficient.
correct_text_lg: Fact: Eky'enkya kye kirungo ekisinga obukulu eri abaana abagenda mu ssomero. Kiwa obwongo okukola obulungi mu kusome. Obushera, ekibala, n'eggi bimala.

correct_text_en: Vaccines are a safe and effective way to protect communities from deadly diseases. Always follow the national immunization schedule provided by the Ministry of Health.
correct_text_lg: Enkingo nnyangu era ziyamba okukuuma abantu ku ndwadde ezitta. Galoberera enteekateeka y'eggwanga ey'okugemba.
symptoms: N/A (Preventative)
prevention: Follow the UNEPI schedule, ensure hygiene during vaccination, and complete all doses.
treatment: Vaccines prevent disease; they are not a cure for active infections.

correct_text_en: HIV is managed through Antiretroviral Therapy (ART), which allows people living with HIV to lead long, healthy lives. Prevention include condom use, PrEP, and safe medical circumcision.
correct_text_lg: HIV ajjanjabibwa n'eddagala lya ART, eriyamba abalina akawuka okuwangaala. Okuziyiza kulimu okukozesa obupiira, PrEP, n'okusala ku basajja mu ngeri ey'ekisawo.
symptoms: Rapid weight loss, recurring fever, extreme tiredness, prolonged swelling of lymph glands.
prevention: ABC (Abstinence, Be Faithful, Condoms), PrEP, PEP, and safe medical circumcision.
treatment: Lifelong ART medication. There is currently no cure, but ART suppresses the virus.

correct_text_en: Malaria is prevented by sleeping under treated mosquito nets, using indoor residual spraying, and clearing stagnant water. Seek immediate testing if you have a fever.
correct_text_lg: Omusujja gw'ensiri guziyizibwa okusula mu butimba obulimu eddagala, okufuuyira mu nju, n'okuggyawo amazzi agalero. Genda okebeberwe mangu ssinga ofuna omusujja.
symptoms: Fever, chills, headache, nausea, and joint pain.
prevention: LLIN nets, IRS spraying, clearing bushes, and removing stagnant water.
treatment: Coartem (ACTs) after testing. Complete the full course even if you feel better.

correct_text_en: Maternal health is prioritized through early ANC visits, facility-based deliveries, and postpartum care. Proper nutrition and rest are essential for a healthy pregnancy.
correct_text_lg: Ebyobulamu bw'abakazi ab'olubuto bikulembezebwa okukeberebwa amangu, okuzaalira mu ddwaliro, n'okufuna obujjanjabi oluvannyuma lw'okuzaala.
symptoms: Danger signs: Vaginal bleeding, severe headache, blurred vision, or swollen feet.
prevention: Regular ANC checks (at least 8), tetanus toxoid injections, and iron supplements.
treatment: Professional obstetric care at a health facility.

correct_text_en: COVID-19 prevention includes vaccination, handwashing with soap, and social distancing when sick. Official guidelines are updated regularly by the Ministry of Health.
correct_text_lg: Okuziyiza COVID-19 kulimu okugemwa, okunaaba mu ngalo n'obusaayi, n'okwewa amabanga. Ebiragiro bitereezebwa bulijjo ekitongole ky'ebyobulamu.
symptoms: Cough, fever, loss of taste/smell, and difficulty breathing.
prevention: Vaccination, wearing masks in crowded places, and handwashing.
treatment: Supportive care, hydration, and medical oxygen in severe cases.

correct_text_en: A balanced diet with local foods like matooke, beans, dodo, and eggs provides essential nutrients for all ages. Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for the first 6 months.
correct_text_lg: Endiisa ennungi erimu emmere ey'awaka ng'ematooke, ebijanjaalo, dodo, n'amagi ewa omubiri ebirungo ebyetaagisa. Okuyonsa bwokka kukkirizibwa mu myezi 6 emisozi.
symptoms: Stunted growth, lack of energy, frequent illness, and pale skin.
prevention: Diverse diet (GO, GROW, GLOW foods) and clean water.
treatment: Nutritional rehabilitation and micronutrient supplementation.

correct_text_en: Proper sanitation involves using clean latrines, handwashing after toilet use, and boiling drinking water to prevent diarrheal diseases.
correct_text_lg: Obuyonjo kulimu okukozesa kaabuyonjo ennyonjo, okunaaba mu ngalo nga wavudde mu kaabuyonjo, n'okufumba amazzi ag'okunywa.
symptoms: Diarrhea, stomach cramps, and vomiting.
prevention: WASH (Water, Sanitation, Hygiene) practices and safe waste disposal.
treatment: Rehydration (ORS) and medical care for severe diarrhea.

correct_text_en: Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) are prevented by consistent condom use and mutual monogamy. Most STIs are curable with proper medical treatment at a health facility.
correct_text_lg: Endwadde ez'ekikaba (STIs) ziyizibwa okukozesa obupiira n'obwesigwa. Endwadde nnyingi ziwona n'obujjanjabi obutuufu mu ddwaliro.
symptoms: Unusual discharge, sores, itching in the genital area, and painful urination.
prevention: Condom use, partner notification, and regular screening.
treatment: Antibiotics or antivirals prescribed by a doctor. Complete the full dose.

myth_text_en: Syphilis can be cured with herbal tea.
correct_text_en: Syphilis is a serious bacterial infection. It CANNOT be cured with herbs. It requires professional medical treatment with antibiotics (usually penicillin). Early treatment is essential to prevent heart and brain damage.
correct_text_lg: Syphilis obulwadde bwa siriimu bwa maanyi. Bugenda na ddagala lya kisawo lyokka (penicillin), si kiti. Jjanjaba mangu okuziyiza obunaku bw'omutima n'obwongo.
symptoms: Painless sores (chancres), skin rash, fever, and swollen glands.
prevention: Safe sex (condoms), mutual monogamy, and regular STI screening.
treatment: Medical antibiotics (Penicillin) prescribed by a health worker.

correct_text_en: Syphilis is a bacterial STI that is fully curable with the right medical treatment. It progresses through stages if left untreated, leading to severe health complications.
correct_text_lg: Syphilis obulwadde obukwatira mu kwegatta naye buwona n'obujjanjabi obutuufu. Ssinga tebujjanjabwa, buleeta obunaku bw'amaanyi.
symptoms: Sores, rashes, fever, and hair loss.
prevention: Condom use and testing.
treatment: Antibiotics (Penicillin) as directed by a doctor.
